source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

3. The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

4. Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

issues: Top-level image_paths and metadata.image_paths reference different image paths.

image_dependency: requires_image=true and text_dominant=false indicate the sample is primarily figure-grounded

Question refers to a specific apparatus layout, labeled points M/N/O, quarter-circular analyzer geometry, field directions, wafer placement, and coordinate axes that are typically resolved from the diagram
Image asset exists and registry_passed=true, so the visual reference is available for use
multi_step: The problem has four linked sub-questions: velocity selector result, magnetic analyzer charge-to-mass selection, electric-only deflection, magnetic-only deflection, and combined-field deflection
Solving requires chaining multiple physics relations across different devices rather than a single lookup or one-step computation
The final part explicitly asks for simultaneous electric and magnetic field effects plus justification, indicating compound reasoning
verifiability: answer_text provides explicit symbolic results for v, q/m, and three coordinate pairs, which are concrete targets for checking
The domain is structured physics with standard relations and stated approximations, making derivations rule-checkable in principle
answer_present=true and registry_passed=true support downstream validation, though the response is multi-part and symbolic rather than a single scalar

risk_flags: Top-level image_paths and metadata.image_paths reference different image paths.

decision_reasons: Question text, primary image, and answer are all present, so the sample retains its core semantics.
Initial scoring is strong: high image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability indicate a valuable figure-grounded physics problem.
Recorded issues are metadata inconsistencies (image path mismatch and answer_type mismatch) rather than missing core assets, so they do not justify downgrading or rejection.

When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper).

In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L.

The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible.
The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small.

node_records.canonical_value:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Determine the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem statement fully specifies the fields, geometry, coordinates, and requested outputs, and the diagram clearly shows the velocity selector, quarter-ring magnetic analyzer, deflection system, and wafer axes. The image is useful and understandable, and the provided answer format matches the four requested parts.

risk_flags: Top-level image_paths and metadata.image_paths reference different image paths.
cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is complete, understandable, and solvable with a usable rewritten question and answer, but the alignment record is marked risky and there is an image-path metadata mismatch. It remains semantically recoverable, so review is preferred over rejection.

field_audit_records.before_value:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

3. The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

4. Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.
